definition: Easily bent or shaped; flexible and yielding without breaking.
example: The pliant willow branches bent gracefully under the weight of snow without snapping.
mnemonic: PLIANT = PLIABLE + ANT — ants bend around obstacles, never rigid.
confusableNote: pliable: nearly synonymous, but pliant more often implies willingness to yield; pliable emphasizes physical flexibility.

definition: Moving or progressing slowly and laboriously; tediously slow and unimaginative.
example: The plodding pace of the investigation frustrated reporters eager for results.
mnemonic: PLOD sounds heavy — imagine heavy boots thudding slowly through mud.

definition: Having many curves and turns; moving in a smooth, winding manner.
example: The sinuous river wound through the valley, doubling back on itself repeatedly.
mnemonic: SINUOUS contains SINUS — a sinus cavity is full of winding curves.

definition: Bending and moving easily and gracefully; lithe and flexible.
example: Years of yoga kept her joints supple well into old age.
mnemonic: SUPPLE — think of a supple whip that bends without breaking.
confusableNote: pliant: pliant can imply yielding to others' will; supple focuses more on physical elasticity.

definition: Moving or developing in an irregular, uncontrolled, or unpredictable direction; difficult to guide.
example: The wayward kite darted erratically across the sky, defying the child's efforts to control it.
mnemonic: WAYWARD = going its own WAY, regardless of direction given.

definition: To diminish in intensity, amount, or degree; to subside or lessen.
example: The storm finally began to abate around midnight, allowing rescue teams to venture out.
mnemonic: ABATE — think of a fish taking the BAIT, then the excitement fades away.
confusableNote: mitigate: mitigate means to make less severe by action; abate suggests a natural lessening or dying down.

definition: To solemnly renounce or reject a belief, claim, or course of action under oath.
example: Facing exile, he was forced to abjure his political beliefs before the court.
mnemonic: AB (away) + JURE (swear) — to swear away your beliefs.
confusableNote: adjure: adjure means to earnestly urge or command; abjure means to renounce — opposite directions.

definition: To formally repeal or abolish a law, treaty, or agreement by authority.
example: The new government moved quickly to abrogate the treaty signed by its predecessor.
mnemonic: AB (away) + ROGATE (ask/decree) — to decree away a law's existence.
confusableNote: arrogate: arrogate means to claim or seize without right; abrogate means to abolish.

definition: To declare legally invalid and void; to cancel or make of no effect.
example: The judge decided to annul the contract because it had been signed under duress.
mnemonic: ANNUL = to NULL — make something null and void.

definition: To ceremonially designate or choose someone for a role or office, often as a successor.
example: The retiring CEO anointed her deputy as the next leader of the company.
mnemonic: Kings were ANOINTED with oil to mark their divine selection for power.

definition: To expel or exile someone from a place, or to drive away a thought or feeling.
example: The king banished the disloyal nobles from the kingdom under penalty of death.
mnemonic: BANISH — BAN someone and make them vanish.

definition: To keep under control; to restrain or curb an impulse, emotion, or action.
example: She had to bridle her temper during the contentious negotiation.
mnemonic: A BRIDLE on a horse controls it — bridle your emotions just the same.
confusableNote: bridle (bristle): to bridle also means to show indignation; context determines which sense applies.

definition: To officially declare as a saint or to treat as authoritative and beyond question.
example: Over time, critics canonized the novelist's early work as the definitive text of the movement.
mnemonic: CANON (authoritative rule) + IZE — to make something part of the sacred canon.

definition: To bring to an end; to stop occurring or being done.
example: The factory agreed to cease all emissions that violated environmental regulations.
mnemonic: CEASE-FIRE — both sides stop shooting; cease = stop completely.

definition: To stop or restrain something from continuing or progressing.
example: The new policy was designed to check the rapid spread of misinformation online.
mnemonic: A CHECK in chess stops the opponent's king — check = halt movement.
confusableNote: check (verify): on the GRE, check means to stop/restrain, not to verify or inspect — a common trap.

definition: To restrict or confine within narrow limits; to define the boundaries of.
example: The constitution circumscribes the power of the executive branch to prevent overreach.
mnemonic: CIRCUM (around) + SCRIBE (draw) — to draw a circle around, limiting space.

definition: The use of force, threats, or intimidation to compel someone to act against their will.
example: The confession was thrown out because the court found evidence of coercion by investigators.
mnemonic: COERCE + ION — to coerce is to force; coercion is the act of forcing.

definition: To keep under control or within limits; to restrain or moderate.
example: The central bank raised interest rates to curb runaway inflation.
mnemonic: A CURB on a street keeps cars from going too far — curb = keep in bounds.

definition: To reduce in extent or quantity; to impose a restriction that cuts something short.
example: Budget cuts forced the university to curtail its study-abroad program significantly.
mnemonic: CURTAIL = cut the TAIL off — shorten or cut back.

definition: To remove from power or office, especially by force or legal means.
example: The military deposed the elected president in an early-morning coup.
mnemonic: DE (down) + POSE (position) — to bring someone down from their position.
confusableNote: depose (legal): in law, depose also means to give sworn testimony — context clarifies the sense.

definition: Exercising absolute, tyrannical power with no regard for others' rights or freedoms.
example: The despotic ruler imprisoned journalists who dared to criticize his regime.
mnemonic: DESPOT = a tyrant; DESPOTIC describes how a despot rules.

definition: To discourage or prevent someone from taking action through fear or doubt.
example: Harsh penalties were introduced to deter would-be offenders from committing fraud.
mnemonic: DETER = DE-TERROR — make something terrifying enough to stop action.

definition: To issue authoritative commands or lay down terms that must be followed.
example: The stronger party was able to dictate the terms of the peace agreement.
mnemonic: DICTATE = DICTATOR behavior — issue commands others must obey.

definition: To prevent or obstruct something by taking advance action before it can occur.
example: She made a preemptive announcement to forestall any damaging rumors from spreading.
mnemonic: FORE (before) + STALL — stall something before it even starts moving.

definition: To abstain from or give up something desirable voluntarily.
example: He chose to forgo the bonus in order to maintain his ethical standards.
mnemonic: FORGO = FOR + GO — let it go, don't take it.
confusableNote: forego: forego means to precede or go before; forgo means to do without — easily confused.

definition: To abandon or renounce someone or something once held dear.
example: He forsook his comfortable career to pursue a life of humanitarian service.
mnemonic: FOR + SAKE — 'for whose sake do you leave?' — you leave it behind.

definition: To hinder or obstruct the movement or progress of someone or something.
example: Heavy fog hampered the rescue operation for most of the morning.
mnemonic: A HAMPER is a basket that traps laundry — hamper = trap and slow things down.
confusableNote: hamper (noun): on the GRE, hamper as a verb means to hinder, not a laundry basket — a common trap.

definition: To make it difficult for someone to do something or for something to happen.
example: Lack of funding continues to hinder scientific progress in the region.
mnemonic: HINDER = put something BEHIND you as an obstacle — block progress.

definition: To delay or obstruct progress by interfering with someone's movement or actions.
example: Bureaucratic red tape impeded the delivery of emergency aid to the affected region.
mnemonic: IM + PEDE (foot) — put a foot in the way to block movement.

definition: A circumstance or obstacle that makes progress or achievement difficult.
example: A lack of transportation remained the greatest impediment to accessing healthcare in rural areas.
mnemonic: IMPEDIMENT = the noun form of IMPEDE — the thing that blocks your feet.

definition: To restrain, hinder, or suppress an action, process, or impulse.
example: Fear of failure often inhibits people from attempting creative endeavors.
mnemonic: IN + HIBIT (have) — to hold back from having or doing something.
confusableNote: prohibit: prohibit means to formally forbid by rule; inhibit means to restrain or hold back, often internally.

definition: To discard or abandon something no longer wanted or useful.
example: The company jettisoned its unprofitable divisions to focus on core operations.
mnemonic: JET + TISON — jets throw cargo overboard in emergencies; jettison = throw out.

definition: Having or showing the authority or dignity of a master or magistrate; commanding and authoritative.
example: The professor delivered a magisterial lecture that left the audience in awe of her command of the subject.
mnemonic: MAGISTER (Latin: master/teacher) + IAL — possessing the manner of a grand master.

definition: To restrain or confine with or as if with handcuffs; to shackle or fetter.
example: Rigid regulations manacled innovation, preventing the industry from adapting to new realities.
mnemonic: MANACLE = MAN +ACLE — a shackle that encircles the hand of a man.

definition: A strict disciplinarian who insists on exact conformity to rules and orders.
example: The new principal was a martinet who enforced every regulation to the letter.
mnemonic: MARTINET — from General Martinet, a famously rigid French drill master.

definition: To make less severe, serious, or painful; to lessen the harmful effects of something.
example: The defendant's remorse was cited as a factor that could mitigate the severity of the sentence.
mnemonic: MITI (gentle) + GATE — open a gentle gate to reduce severity.
confusableNote: militate: militate means to have a strong effect against something; mitigate means to lessen — easily confused.

definition: To make legally void and of no effect; to cancel out or neutralize.
example: The appeals court decided to nullify the lower court's ruling on procedural grounds.
mnemonic: NULLIFY = make NULL — reduce to null, nothing, zero.

definition: To remove a difficulty or need before it arises; to prevent by anticipatory action.
example: Installing better drainage systems would obviate the need for costly flood relief each year.
mnemonic: OB (in the way) + VIA (road) — clear the road of obstacles before you reach them.
confusableNote: obviate vs. mitigate: obviate removes the problem entirely; mitigate merely lessens it.

definition: To block or obstruct a passage, opening, or line of sight.
example: A blood clot occluded the artery, cutting off blood flow to the heart.
mnemonic: OC + CLUDE (close) — to close off completely, as in occluded artery.

definition: To leave out or exclude something, either deliberately or through oversight.
example: The editor chose to omit the most inflammatory passages from the published version.
mnemonic: OMIT — O MIT: something MIssed ouT — left out entirely.

definition: To drive out or expel someone from a position of power or place of belonging.
example: Board members voted to oust the CEO following the financial scandal.
mnemonic: OUST sounds like OUT — to push someone OUT of their position.

definition: To exceed, surpass, or leave far behind in growth, performance, or achievement.
example: Demand for electric vehicles has begun to outstrip the supply of charging infrastructure.
mnemonic: OUT + STRIP — strip past everyone else and go OUT ahead.

definition: To make something impossible in advance; to prevent from happening by prior action.
example: His prior criminal record precluded him from applying for the government position.
mnemonic: PRE (before) + CLUDE (close) — close the door before someone can enter.

definition: To prove more powerful or widespread; to triumph over opposition or to be the dominant force.
example: Despite setbacks, the reformers' vision ultimately prevailed over decades of resistance.
mnemonic: PRE + VAIL (be strong) — to be strongest and gain the upper hand.
